researchObjective: JSXGraph canvas enhancement for palantir-math Sequencer — real-time functiongraph drag, intersection tracking, absolute value fold visualization, teaching annotations

hypotheses.statement: AtomicWrite-first + StateReducer (DC5-06) + TypeBox (SchemaValidatorInterface) is the optimal architecture for this DATA/LOGIC/ACTION structure because the snapshot model centers BunStateFile as the canonical DATA artifact, TypeBox propagates to JSON Schema/OpenAPI surfaces via ForwardProp, and StateReducer preserves history for LEARN via BackwardProp.
hypotheses.dlaJustification: DATA-dominant: BunStateFile and StateSchemaDefinition (TypeBox) are primary runtime artifacts. LOGIC: StateReducer reads snapshots to compute derived values without log replay. ACTION: AtomicWriteAction is the single mutation path. SECURITY: FileSystemAccessPolicy governs snapshot directory. LEARN: StateChangeAuditLog captures write events independently.
testCriteria: Process kill during Bun.write() does not produce a corrupt state.json
TypeBox schema integrates with CLI help text generation without additional tooling
StateReducer can compute derived properties from snapshot chain without replaying all history
architectureImplications: State is always a consistent snapshot — reads are fast (no log replay needed)
Crash during write may corrupt snapshot — requires temp-file rename pattern or post-crash repair
TypeBox JSON Schema output enables OpenAPI binding (ForwardProp to API contract layer)
StateChangeAuditLog tracks write events separately from the snapshot itself

devcon5Alignment.DDD: BunStateFile/AtomicWriteAction named per Bun domain vocabulary. TypeBox schema 'StateSchemaDefinition' per state domain concept. No violations.
devcon5Alignment.DRY: Single write path (AtomicWriteAction). SchemaValidatorInterface abstracts TypeBox/Valibot/Zod.
devcon5Alignment.OCP: SchemaValidatorInterface open for new validators. AtomicWriteAction extensible (path/format) without modifying core.
devcon5Alignment.PECS: SchemaValidatorInterface: consumes unknown (super) → produces T (extends). Correct.

propagation.forward: BunStateFile (ontology) → StateFileContract (contract) → Bun.file().json() adapter (backend) → CLI 'state read' command | status: partial
propagation.backward: write() call → StateChangeAuditLog entry (lineage) → write success/latency evaluation → retry strategy refinement | status: partial

hypotheses.statement: AppendLog-first + StateReducer (DC5-06 event sourcing) + Zod (SchemaValidatorInterface) is the optimal architecture for this DATA/LOGIC/ACTION structure because the event-sourcing model makes StateChangeAuditLog the primary LEARN artifact, provides inherent crash safety (log entries are immutable once flushed), and TypeScript-ergonomic Zod validation fits CLI-first development.
hypotheses.dlaJustification: LEARN-dominant: StateChangeAuditLog (AppendLogAction's output) is the primary source of truth. LOGIC: StateReducer replays log entries to derive current state. ACTION: AppendLogAction is the only mutation path — no state file is ever overwritten. SECURITY: FileSystemAccessPolicy governs log directory. DATA: BunStateFile is a compaction artifact, not primary.
testCriteria: Log replay from 1000 mutation events produces correct current state
Log growth does not cause unacceptable startup latency (< 100ms for 10,000 entries)
FileSink .flush() call guarantees no entries lost on process termination
architectureImplications: Inherently crash-safe: appended entries are immutable — no partial-write corruption risk for existing entries
Read path requires log replay via StateReducer — latency grows with log size without compaction
Compaction snapshots (AtomicWriteAction) needed periodically to bound startup latency
StateChangeAuditLog doubles as both primary data store and LEARN artifact — LEARN domain becomes central

devcon5Alignment.DDD: AppendLogAction named per event-sourcing vocabulary. Zod 'StateSchemaDefinition' accurate but less aligned with OpenAPI surfaces than TypeBox.
devcon5Alignment.DRY: Single append path (AppendLogAction). Compaction reuses AtomicWriteAction — DRY by composition.
devcon5Alignment.OCP: StateReducer replay logic closed to log format changes. AppendLogAction open for new event types via event.type discriminator.
devcon5Alignment.PECS: SchemaValidatorInterface still applies. StateReducer: produces T (focus value) from LogEntry[] (preserved history) — Producer pattern.

propagation.forward: StateChangeAuditLog (ontology) → AuditContract (contract) → FileSink adapter (backend) → CLI 'state history' query surface | status: partial
propagation.backward: append() call → StateChangeAuditLog record (lineage) → log completeness evaluation → flush strategy refinement | status: partial

hypotheses.userPreference: PREFERRED — user direction: maximize JSXGraph native APIs. Custom code ONLY for what JSXGraph cannot natively provide.
hypotheses.pr90Context: PR #90 merged: AbsDragBehavior (translate-source-before-outer-abs) is partially implemented. This H-A hypothesis extends that work with native intersection, native derivative, native dynamic text elements, and native reducedUpdate optimization.
hypotheses.statement: Maximize JSXGraph native APIs across all enhanced features — use native Intersection element (v1.12.1 improved), native Derivative element, native dynamic Text (auto-updating), native OtherIntersection (multiple points), board.reducedUpdate=true (60fps), board.suspendUpdate/unsuspendUpdate (batch repaint). Custom code ONLY for the three gaps JSXGraph cannot handle natively: (1) |f(x)| fold visualization ghost curve + fold boundary (no native abs-fold element), (2) teaching annotation toggle overlay, (3) piecewise/parametric drag semantics. Builds directly on PR #90's translate-source-before-outer-abs foundation.
hypotheses.dlaJustification: DATA-dominant: IntersectionPoint and CoordinateLabel auto-derive from parentPoint.Y() position — zero manual sync needed. LOGIC: DependencyGraph IS JSXGraph's native _dep system — NativeDependencyChain maps directly to JSXGraph element graph, no custom reasoning layer. ACTION: DragCascadeUpdate is reduced to parentPoint.on('drag') handler — all cascade is JSXGraph-internal. LEARN: element.on('drag') provides a clean event boundary for InteractionTelemetry capture with per-element granularity.
hypotheses.pr90Context: PR #90 MERGED: AbsDragBehavior (translate-source-before-outer-abs) now implemented in functiongraphDragSemantics.ts. GraphFamilyProfile outer-abs recognition added to graphFamily.ts. AbsFoldVisualizationUpdate (ACTION) added to jsxGraphRenderer.ts. H-A builds DIRECTLY on this foundation — migration for outer-abs drag semantics is COMPLETE, remaining work is: fold LINE visualization, IntersectionPoint real-time recompute, dynamic CoordinateLabels.
testCriteria: board.create('intersection', [fg1, fg2, 0]) returns a live-updating IntersectionPoint as fg1's parentPoint is dragged — no manual board.update() needed
board.reducedUpdate=true maintains >= 60fps with 10 active dependent elements during functiongraph drag
CoordinateLabel text updates in real time (<16ms) when parentPoint.Y() changes during drag
Absolute value ghost curve (Math.abs(f_raw(x)+parentPoint.Y())) re-renders correctly on each drag frame
TracePath custom dataX/dataY array update works via board.on('update') even under native dependency cascade
architectureImplications: All dependent elements declared at board creation time with JSXGraph-native closures over parentPoint — zero manual wiring needed
board.reducedUpdate=true enables significant performance optimization: only parentPoint + direct dependents recomputed per frame
Migration required: current raw DOM addEventListener must be replaced with JSXGraph parentPoint pattern in functiongraphDragSemantics.ts
Intersection element (board.create('intersection')) auto-updates — no custom solver needed as primary path; JXG.Math.Numerics as fallback for complex intersections
ORCH-10 compliance: all annotation elements are explicitly declared board elements, not ad-hoc DOM mutations
Tracecurve (native) not directly usable (requires Glider mover) — custom board.on('update') push to dataX/dataY still needed for TracePath

devcon5Alignment.DDD: parentPoint named per JSXGraph domain vocabulary (JXG.Point). functiongraph → parentPoint.Y() follows JSXGraph draggable exponential function wiki pattern exactly. No invented abstractions — pure JSXGraph idioms.
devcon5Alignment.DRY: Single drag handler on parentPoint — zero manual element.update() calls in cascade. board.reducedUpdate=true eliminates all N-element manual update duplication.
devcon5Alignment.OCP: Add new dependent elements (integral display, second derivative, tangent line) without modifying drag handler or IntersectionResolverInterface — just declare with parentPoint closures. Fully open for extension.
devcon5Alignment.PECS: IntersectionResolverInterface: consumes [JXG.Curve, JXG.Curve] (super — abstract curve type), produces IntersectionCoordinate {x,y} (extends — specific result). DragBehaviorInterface: consumes DragEvent (super), produces DragState ty/tx (extends).

propagation.forward: IntersectionResolverInterface (ontology) → NativeIntersectionResolver: board.create('intersection', [fg1, fg2, 0]) (contract+backend) → JSXGraph _dep auto-cascade → CoordinateLabel auto-update → JSXGraphCanvas live display (frontend) | status: HEALTHY — native cascade handles all steps once board elements declared
propagation.backward: parentPoint.on('drag') → drag frame timestamps + ty delta in InteractionTelemetry.dragFrames (lineage) → frame rate + reducedUpdate effectiveness evaluation → DragBehaviorInterface / board.reducedUpdate toggle refinement (refinement) → DragBehaviorInterface algorithm note update (ontology) | status: partial — telemetry capture hook not yet declared in jsxGraphRenderer.ts

hypotheses.architectureGapRisk: MEDIUM — requires migration from raw DOM drag to JSXGraph parentPoint pattern. Risk: regression in existing drag behavior (snap-to-grid, tangency assist) during migration. Mitigation: incremental migration per graph family using DragBehaviorInterface.

hypotheses.userPreference: ALTERNATIVE — competing hypothesis, NOT the user's preferred direction. Present as rigorous alternative to Hypothesis A.
hypotheses.pr90Context: PR #90 merged: raw DOM drag still in place (H-B preserves it). board.on('update') pattern adds observer on top of existing raw DOM drag. However PR #90's fold-aware rendering was done via jsxGraphRenderer.ts updates, not board.on('update') observer — H-B would need to retrofit into this pattern.
hypotheses.statement: Keep raw DOM addEventListener for functiongraph drag; build explicit board observer architecture — board.on('update') fires every drag frame, triggering JXG.Math.Numerics.root() intersection solver (warm start from previous intersection x), explicit BatchBoardUpdate for all N dependent elements (CoordinateLabels, ghost curves, fold boundary), and custom TracePath array push. Maximum control over computation ordering, telemetry capture, and failure recovery. Zero migration cost. Trades JSXGraph-native elegance for explicit custom control.
hypotheses.dlaJustification: LOGIC-dominant: IntersectionSolver (JXG.Math.Numerics.root) is the critical LOGIC layer — custom warm-start strategy enables <1ms re-rooting for polynomial graphs. DependencyGraph is explicit custom DAG — full control over update ordering. ACTION: DragCascadeUpdate = board.on('update') observer callback containing all N element updates. DATA: CoordinateLabel and IntersectionPoint explicitly populated by observer — full control over formatting and failure handling. LEARN: board.on('update') exposes every frame for fine-grained InteractionTelemetry capture (solver iteration counts, individual element update durations).
testCriteria: JXG.Math.Numerics.root(h, warmStart) where h(x)=f(x)-g(x) completes in <1ms for linear, quadratic, and abs functiongraph pairs during drag
board.on('update') callback completes full intersection recompute + label update + fold geometry update within 16ms frame budget
Warm start strategy (previous intersection x as initial guess) reduces solver iterations vs cold start by >= 50%
board.suspendUpdate() + unsuspendUpdate() reduces visible DOM flicker vs per-element update in 10+ element scenario
InteractionTelemetry captures solver iteration counts and frame durations with < 0.1ms overhead per frame
architectureImplications: board.on('update') observer grows as dependent element count increases — DRY risk at 5+ elements (rule of three triggered)
board.reducedUpdate=true CANNOT be used: reducedUpdate bypasses the update chain, suppressing the board.on('update') observer trigger — mutually exclusive with the H-B architecture
Zero migration cost: raw DOM addEventListener stays in place; board.on('update') is purely additive
Full telemetry control: solver iteration counts, individual element latency, and frame budget breakdowns accessible in observer callback
ORCH-10 compliance: all annotation elements must still be declared board elements, not DOM mutations — constraint applies equally to H-A and H-B
DDD partial violation: 'board observer architecture' is non-JSXGraph vocabulary — introduces custom concept not found in JSXGraph docs (§DC5-05 flag)

devcon5Alignment.DDD: PARTIAL VIOLATION: 'board observer' and 'explicit dependency graph' introduce non-JSXGraph domain vocabulary (§DC5-05 flag). JXG.Math.Numerics.root is JSXGraph-native vocabulary. Raw DOM addEventListener is non-JSXGraph vocabulary already present in codebase (flagged in decision-log).
devcon5Alignment.DRY: RISK: board.on('update') observer accumulates element update calls as element count grows. Rule of three: at 3+ distinct element types in observer → abstraction required (DependencyGraph traversal). Without abstraction, observer becomes a DRY violation by element #5.
devcon5Alignment.OCP: VIOLATION for base case: adding new dependent elements REQUIRES modifying board.on('update') callback. Only resolved if DependencyGraph traversal is added (which pushes toward a hybrid H-A/H-B architecture).
devcon5Alignment.PECS: ANTI-PATTERN risk: board.on('update') mixes SENSE (read element positions) + DECIDE (run solver) + ACT (update labels) + LEARN (push telemetry) in one callback. Violates separation that PECS interfaces would enforce. Can be mitigated by decomposing the callback into typed handler functions.

propagation.forward: IntersectionResolverInterface (ontology) → NewtonRaphsonResolver: JXG.Math.Numerics.root(h, warmStart) (contract+backend) → board.on('update') explicit call → CoordinateLabel.setText() mutation → JSXGraphCanvas SVG repaint (frontend) | status: PARTIAL — explicit contract layer between solver and observer not yet declared; observer callback is an implicit dependency hub
propagation.backward: board.on('update') frame → explicit frame timestamp + solver iterations push to InteractionTelemetry (lineage) → solver performance + warmStart effectiveness evaluation → Newton warmStart initial guess strategy refinement → IntersectionResolverInterface algorithm note update (ontology) | status: partial — observation hook requires explicit declaration in architecture

hypotheses.architectureGapRisk: HIGH — board.on('update') + raw DOM addEventListener creates an implicit coordination problem: DOM drag events and JSXGraph board updates may fire in different frames, creating potential race conditions in ty synchronization. This is an architectural fact, not an evidence gap.

scenarios.description: Most likely outcome: Bun.write() + TypeBox + StateReducer provides a stable, performant CLI state management solution for typical usage (single-agent, < 1MB state files, infrequent writes).

assumptions.text: Bun.write() provides adequate write performance via optimized syscalls (not necessarily atomic, but fast)
TypeBox validation at ~8ms/100k iterations is negligible overhead for CLI state files (< 1000 reads/writes per session)
StateReducer (DC5-06) derives current state from snapshot without requiring log replay
FileSystemAccessPolicy (POSIX chmod 600) provides sufficient access control for single-user CLI tool

contradictions.claim: Bun.write() atomicity unconfirmed — 'optimized syscalls' does not explicitly guarantee atomic write behavior on process termination
contradictions.resolution: ROUND 2: c-s1-05 'optimized syscalls' on POSIX Linux implies write() syscall which is atomic for small file writes. Resolved by POSIX inference. NextExperiment: confirm Bun.write() atomic behavior with kill -9 test.

evaluationRationale.evidenceFit: Core assumptions backed by tier-1 Bun docs and official DC5-06; TypeBox performance from tier-3 blog only
evaluationRationale.implementationDifficulty: Straightforward — proven Bun.write + TypeBox pattern, clear docs, ~1-2 weeks
evaluationRationale.riskSeverity: Minor — Bun.write atomicity gap is low-probability; workaround via temp-file rename available
evaluationRationale.reversibility: Mostly reversible — SchemaValidatorInterface swap in days; snapshot→log-first requires refactor but data preserved
evaluationRationale.timeToValue: Days — working prototype with Bun.write + TypeBox + minimal StateReducer in < 1 week
evaluationRationale.governanceCompliance: CLI tool, no regulated data, POSIX permissions sufficient
evaluationRationale.ecosystemMaturity: Bun 1.x stable 2+ years; TypeBox actively maintained; both production-ready
evaluationRationale.dlaFit: All 5 domains covered with classified objects; SECURITY based on inference from Bun path semantics
evaluationRationale.forwardPropHealth: Backend propagation designed; CLI command surface not yet declared; TypeBox→JSON Schema path documented not implemented
evaluationRationale.agentComposability: Core read/write operations composable by agents; concurrent multi-agent writes require custom locking or sequential protocol

scenarios.description: Optimistic outcome: gap-s1-01 resolved favorably for Bun.write(), TypeBox JSON Schema integrates seamlessly with CLI tooling, and AtomicWriteAction achieves true crash safety via OS-level guarantees.

assumptions.text: Bun.write() confirmed atomic: internally uses O_TMPFILE + rename or equivalent POSIX atomic write pattern
TypeBox JSON Schema output integrates with CLI help text and OpenAPI surface without additional tooling
StateReducer read-time derivation stays < 5ms for typical snapshot chains (< 100 versions)
SchemaValidatorInterface enables seamless TypeBox→Valibot swap if bundle constraint emerges (OCP)

evaluationRationale.evidenceFit: Assumption a3 (StateReducer latency < 5ms) is inference-only; a1 assumes gap-s1-01 resolved favorably without direct evidence
evaluationRationale.implementationDifficulty: Trivial — existing patterns, clear Bun docs, TypeBox well-documented, < 1 week
evaluationRationale.riskSeverity: Negligible — crash safety guaranteed if Bun.write atomic; TypeBox OCP enables risk-free validator swap
evaluationRationale.reversibility: Fully reversible — SchemaValidatorInterface drop-in swap; snapshot format migration scripted via StateReducer
evaluationRationale.dlaFit: All 5 domains cleanly covered with evidence-backed objects; ForwardProp + BackwardProp paths declared
evaluationRationale.forwardPropHealth: TypeBox→JSON Schema→OpenAPI chain documented; CLI command surface gap is minor (easily added)
evaluationRationale.agentComposability: Most operations composable; concurrent write coordination is minor adapter (lock token pattern)

scenarios.description: Pessimistic outcome: Bun.write() atomicity fails on SIGKILL, producing a corrupt state.json that breaks the entire CLI tool until manually repaired.

assumptions.text: Bun.write() uses in-place overwrite (not temp-file rename) — SIGKILL during write produces partial state.json
StateSchemaDefinition (TypeBox) validation correctly detects corrupt state.json on next load
No automatic repair mechanism exists — user must manually delete and reinitialize state
Corruption occurs in production after 1000+ hours of use (low but non-zero probability)

contradictions.claim: c-s1-05 says Bun.write uses 'optimized syscalls' — on Linux, write() to a file followed by fsync() is effectively atomic for power-failure scenarios, but not for SIGKILL
contradictions.resolution: ROUND 2: POSIX write() is NOT atomic for SIGKILL — kernel may flush partial buffer. However, Bun.write() with small JSON (< 4KB) fits in a single write() syscall which IS atomic at the block level. Resolution: partial — small state files likely safe; large state files at risk. Downgrade severity for typical CLI use.

evaluationRationale.evidenceFit: Assumptions a3 and a4 (no repair, corruption frequency) have no evidence IDs — scenario is partially speculative
evaluationRationale.implementationDifficulty: Moderate — mitigations (temp-file rename, corruption detection, auto-repair) require custom code, ~2-4 weeks
evaluationRationale.riskSeverity: Severe — state corruption = CLI tool fails until manual intervention; data loss for multi-agent sessions
evaluationRationale.reversibility: Partial — can add temp-file pattern without full rewrite; but corrupted state files require manual recovery
evaluationRationale.timeToValue: 2-4 weeks to implement safe write-and-rename pattern
evaluationRationale.governanceCompliance: No regulated data — corruption is user inconvenience, not compliance failure
evaluationRationale.ecosystemMaturity: Bun ecosystem mature; but atomic write patterns not documented in Bun official docs
evaluationRationale.dlaFit: Core D/L/A clear; crash recovery path crosses ACTION/DATA boundary — not cleanly classified
evaluationRationale.forwardPropHealth: Corrupt state breaks StateFileContract → downstream propagation undefined; ForwardProp chain broken at DATA layer
evaluationRationale.agentComposability: Agents cannot compose against a corrupt state tool without error recovery protocol; not composable by default

scenarios.description: Adversarial: Two Kosmos agent threads write state simultaneously via AtomicWriteAction with no coordination protocol — last write wins, first write's data is silently lost. Discovered in Phase 3 when Kosmos Agent Teams adds concurrent agents.

assumptions.text: Multiple Kosmos agents run concurrently and call AtomicWriteAction simultaneously on the same state file path
Bun.write() provides no built-in file locking — concurrent writes race at OS level
Last-write-wins: second agent's write succeeds but silently overwrites first agent's state changes
TypeBox validation succeeds (valid schema) but semantics are wrong (stale or merged incorrectly)

contradictions.claim: File locking at OS level (flock/fcntl) IS available to Bun via Bun.ffi or OS bindings — Bun just doesn't expose a high-level API for it
contradictions.resolution: UNRESOLVABLE: While OS-level locking IS possible, the core claim stands — Bun.write() as called in AtomicWriteAction provides NO inherent locking. Implementing locking requires custom code (OS bindings, advisory lock file). This is an irreconcilable tension: the ontology's AtomicWriteAction as currently designed does not solve concurrent access. Split into two sub-scenarios would be: (a) without-locking (this scenario) and (b) with-locking (mitigation). For smoke test: keep as unresolvable since fixing this requires AtomicWriteAction redesign.

evaluationRationale.evidenceFit: Concurrent write failure mode is inference; core Bun.write behavior from official docs; locking absence inferred from docs
evaluationRationale.implementationDifficulty: Difficult — adding file locking requires OS bindings or advisory lock file pattern; 1-2 months to implement safely
evaluationRationale.riskSeverity: Critical — silent state corruption with no error until next validation; in multi-agent Kosmos, this is a production safety issue
evaluationRationale.reversibility: Partial — can add locking layer without full architecture rewrite; but existing corrupted states unrecoverable
evaluationRationale.governanceCompliance: No regulated data — data loss is user inconvenience, not compliance failure
evaluationRationale.ecosystemMaturity: Bun file locking APIs not documented — emerging area; limited production examples
evaluationRationale.dlaFit: Concurrent write failure crosses ACTION/SECURITY boundaries — D/L/A boundary ambiguity for race conditions
evaluationRationale.forwardPropHealth: Race condition breaks StateFileContract atomicity assumption — ForwardProp chain disconnected at ACTION layer
evaluationRationale.agentComposability: Agents competing for write access with no coordination protocol = not composable; architecture fails at Phase 3 (AI-First)

scenarios.description: Most likely outcome: FileSink append + StateReducer replay + Zod validation provides reliable, crash-safe CLI state management with the StateChangeAuditLog as a first-class LEARN artifact.

assumptions.text: FileSink .flush() guarantees log entry durability before process exit when called explicitly
StateReducer replay of log entries produces correct current state (Reducer property: reduce(events) = focus_value)
Zod v4 validation at ~180ms/100k (~1.8μs/call) is negligible for CLI state files (< 100 validations per session)
Log growth is bounded by periodic compaction via AtomicWriteAction (snapshot), resetting replay from compaction checkpoint

contradictions.claim: gap-s1-01: FileSink flush guarantees not confirmed in docs reviewed — partial log entries possible on SIGKILL
contradictions.resolution: ROUND 1: c-s1-06 explicitly documents FileSink .flush() and .end() methods — developer MUST call flush() before exit to guarantee durability. If called, guarantee is provided. Contradiction RESOLVED: AppendLogAction must call .flush() before returning. This is a design requirement, not an inherent limitation.

contradictions.claim: c-s1-07: Zod v4 ~180ms/100k vs TypeBox ~8ms — H2 chose slower validator, which could matter at scale
contradictions.resolution: ROUND 1: 180ms/100k = 1.8μs per call. CLI session with < 100 validations = < 180μs total validation overhead. This is negligible. For Zod's ergonomic TypeScript inference (key advantage for CLI-first dev), this trade-off is clearly justified. Contradiction RESOLVED: Zod performance is acceptable at CLI scale.

evaluationRationale.evidenceFit: FileSink and Bun.write from official docs; StateReducer from official DC5-06; Zod perf from tier-3 blog; all core assumptions backed
evaluationRationale.implementationDifficulty: Moderate — event sourcing + log replay adds architectural complexity; compaction strategy requires design; ~2-4 weeks
evaluationRationale.riskSeverity: Minor — explicit .flush() resolves gap-s1-01; log entries immutable; primary risk is compaction timing
evaluationRationale.reversibility: Partial — switching log-first to snapshot-first requires log migration script; data preserved but architecture refactor
evaluationRationale.timeToValue: 1-2 weeks — FileSink API simple; StateReducer replay straightforward for small logs
evaluationRationale.ecosystemMaturity: Bun 1.x stable; Zod v4 released; FileSink API documented in official Bun docs
evaluationRationale.dlaFit: All 5 domains explicitly mapped; StateChangeAuditLog as LEARN artifact is ideal DC5-06 alignment; cleanest D/L/A of all 8 scenarios
evaluationRationale.forwardPropHealth: Audit log contract designed; CLI 'state history' surface documented; StateReducer read path not fully implemented
evaluationRationale.agentComposability: AppendLogAction inherently safe for concurrent agents (append-only, no read-modify-write cycle); agents can append independently

scenarios.description: Optimistic outcome: FileSink WAL semantics provide strong durability guarantees, StateReducer replay is fast (< 10ms for 10k entries on Bun), and the append-only log enables perfect agent composability without coordination overhead.

assumptions.text: FileSink .flush() + .end() provides WAL (Write-Ahead Log) semantics — entries durable before exit
Bun StateReducer replay achieves < 10ms for 10,000 log entries (Bun runtime is ~3× faster than Node.js)
Append-only log enables lock-free concurrent agent writes — two agents can append simultaneously without coordination
Compaction runs automatically after every N entries, bounding startup latency to O(1) amortized

evaluationRationale.evidenceFit: a2 (Bun replay latency < 10ms for 10k) is inference only — no benchmark; a3 (lock-free concurrent appends) assumes POSIX append atomicity which may not hold for large writes
evaluationRationale.implementationDifficulty: Straightforward once FileSink semantics confirmed; compaction is additional work but well-understood pattern
evaluationRationale.riskSeverity: Negligible — append-only log has no overwrite risk; flush guarantees durability
evaluationRationale.reversibility: Mostly reversible — log is self-documenting; can migrate to snapshot-first via StateReducer replay + final snapshot
evaluationRationale.timeToValue: 1-2 weeks — event sourcing pattern well-established in TypeScript ecosystem
evaluationRationale.ecosystemMaturity: Bun 1.x; Zod v4; FileSink documented; all stable
evaluationRationale.dlaFit: All domains cleanly covered; StateChangeAuditLog as primary LEARN entity is ideal alignment with DC5-06 Reducer preserved history
evaluationRationale.forwardPropHealth: AuditContract CLI surface documented; StateReducer as query surface exposable as agent tool; minor gap in CLI command schema
evaluationRationale.agentComposability: Append-only = no coordination needed between agents; each agent appends independently; StateReducer handles aggregation; perfect Phase 3 alignment

scenarios.description: Pessimistic outcome: Log replay latency degrades with log growth, compaction itself has atomicity risk, and Bun FileSink provides weaker-than-expected flush guarantees for high-frequency writes.

assumptions.text: FileSink flush latency is high under heavy load — explicit .flush() call blocks for > 100ms per write
StateReducer log replay exceeds 500ms startup latency for 50,000+ entries without compaction
Compaction snapshot (Bun.write) is not atomic — compaction failure produces a corrupt snapshot + truncated log
Zod v4 schema validation performance degrades at large state sizes (> 10MB) in ways not reflected in 100k simple iteration benchmark

contradictions.claim: c-s1-06 documents explicit .flush() as provided — this means flush behavior IS defined, suggesting guarantee IS available
contradictions.resolution: ROUND 1: c-s1-06 shows .flush() exists and is documented. Flush latency concern is about implementation, not availability. PARTIALLY RESOLVED: flush exists; high-latency concern is evidence gap not an irreconcilable contradiction. Update: assumption a1 revised to 'flush latency under heavy write load is unconfirmed'.

contradictions.claim: No evidence for StateReducer replay latency at 50,000 entries — this is a gap that NextExperiment should resolve
contradictions.resolution: ROUND 2: Remains unresolved — no Bun replay benchmark available in smoke-001 session. NextExperiment created: 'bun bench with 50k StateReducer events'. Flags for evaluator.

evaluationRationale.evidenceFit: Assumptions a2 and a3 have no evidence; a1 partially resolved; scenario is significantly speculative
evaluationRationale.implementationDifficulty: Difficult — WAL-level guarantees, compaction atomicity, startup latency budget enforcement all require custom work
evaluationRationale.riskSeverity: Severe — slow startup + potential compaction failure = user-visible system failure
evaluationRationale.reversibility: Difficult — log-first is an architectural commitment; migration to snapshot-first requires tooling and could corrupt state
evaluationRationale.timeToValue: 1-2 months to implement production-safe log + compaction + latency bounds
evaluationRationale.ecosystemMaturity: FileSink at production scale for large logs: limited production examples in Bun ecosystem
evaluationRationale.dlaFit: Compaction failure crosses ACTION/DATA boundary; crash recovery path not cleanly classified
evaluationRationale.forwardPropHealth: Slow replay breaks StateReducer's implicit contract with CLI; ForwardProp chain stalls at startup
evaluationRationale.agentComposability: Agents cannot compose against a slow-starting or compaction-failed state tool

scenarios.description: Adversarial: Log file grows to 1GB+ after a long-running Kosmos session with 100,000+ state mutations. StateReducer replay becomes a 30-second startup penalty. The compaction trigger itself fails (Bun.write not atomic), leaving a corrupted snapshot and a now-truncated log.

assumptions.text: A long-running Kosmos session produces 100,000+ state mutation events at ~1KB each = ~100MB log file
StateReducer replay of 100,000 entries takes > 30 seconds on standard hardware
Compaction trigger fires during peak load — Bun.write() for compaction snapshot is interrupted by SIGKILL mid-write
After failed compaction: snapshot is corrupt, original log is partially truncated — no recovery path without manual intervention

contradictions.claim: c-s1-03: DC5-06 Reducers aggregate 'efficiently' — but 'efficiently' not defined for unbounded history; 100k events may still be fast on Bun
contradictions.resolution: UNRESOLVABLE without benchmark: 'efficiently' is relative. Log-first architectures fundamentally require either bounded log size (via compaction) or fast replay. At 100k entries, the efficiency claim cannot be assumed without empirical data. This is an architectural fact: unbounded append-only logs require compaction strategies. Marked unresolvable for architecture selection purposes.

contradictions.claim: Compaction strategy (Bun.write for snapshot) introduces the same atomicity concern as H1 (AtomicWrite-first)
contradictions.resolution: UNRESOLVABLE: Both H1 and H2 require Bun.write() for snapshot persistence. H2's append-only nature makes the log safe, but compaction introduces H1's atomicity risk as a dependency. This is irreconcilable: any system that needs compaction inherits H1's worst-case risk. H2 is only strictly safer than H1 if compaction is never needed — which requires bounding log size to startup-time-acceptable levels without Bun.write.

evaluationRationale.evidenceFit: a2 and a4 have zero evidence; a1 uses c-s1-06 as proxy for log growth rate (imprecise); scenario is mostly speculative
evaluationRationale.implementationDifficulty: Extremely hard — requires log size monitoring, bounded compaction, corruption detection, manual recovery tooling, startup latency SLA enforcement
evaluationRationale.riskSeverity: Critical — 30-second startup freeze + corrupted state after compaction failure = production system failure requiring manual intervention
evaluationRationale.reversibility: Irreversible — log-first architecture commitment means migrating away requires significant tooling investment; existing data may be in log format only
evaluationRationale.timeToValue: 1-2 months before production-safe log management is implemented
evaluationRationale.governanceCompliance: No regulated data but tool unavailability has operational consequences
evaluationRationale.ecosystemMaturity: FileSink at GB scale: no documented production use cases in Bun ecosystem reviewed
evaluationRationale.dlaFit: Log growth + compaction failure crosses all D/L/A boundaries — recovery path not classified; semantic drift in the architecture itself
evaluationRationale.forwardPropHealth: Startup freeze and corrupt state break ForwardProp chain entirely — ontology → contract → backend → CLI all disconnected
evaluationRationale.agentComposability: Agents cannot compose against a frozen CLI tool; no recovery protocol defined in ontology; Phase 3 complete failure

scenarios.description: Standard adoption: migrate functiongraph drag to JSXGraph-native parentPoint + _dep chain. Native Intersection element auto-updates CoordinateLabels. board.reducedUpdate=true maintains 60fps for 8 dependent elements. TracePath handled via hybrid board.on('update') array push.

assumptions.text: board.create('intersection', [fg1, fg2, 0]) returns a live-updating IntersectionPoint auto-connected to functiongraph's native _dep system — no manual board.update() needed
board.reducedUpdate=true limits per-frame computation to parentPoint + direct dependents only, maintaining 60fps with 8 dependent elements on SVG renderer
CoordinateLabel (JXG.Text with function() callback over parentPoint.Y()) updates in real time within 16ms when parentPoint.Y() changes

DragBehaviorInterface enables incremental migration from raw DOM addEventListener without regressing snap-to-grid and tangency assist. PR #90 already implemented AbsDragBehavior — pattern is proven for outer-abs case.

contradictions.claim: ev-009 confirms Tracecurve requires a Glider as mover, conflicting with 'all 8 dependents auto-cascade via native _dep' — TracePath cannot use native tracecurve element
contradictions.resolution: ROUND 1: TracePath and JSXGraph tracecurve are different mechanisms. TracePath can use board.on('update') to push dataX/dataY arrays (custom approach) while all other dependents cascade natively. This is a documented hybrid: 7 native + 1 custom. Exception is scoped, does not invalidate the core architecture. contradictionStatus: resolved.

evaluationRationale.evidenceFit: Core assumptions backed by tier-1/2 JSXGraph docs (ev-001/ev-005/ev-007); reducedUpdate scope at 8+ dependents is minor gap
evaluationRationale.implementationDifficulty: Moderate — migration from raw DOM addEventListener to parentPoint pattern in functiongraphDragSemantics.ts, ~2-4 weeks
evaluationRationale.riskSeverity: Minor — DragBehaviorInterface provides incremental migration path; snap-to-grid regression risk is manageable
evaluationRationale.reversibility: Mostly reversible — DragBehaviorInterface abstraction layer enables revert within days; TracePath hybrid is additive
evaluationRationale.timeToValue: 2-4 weeks — migration non-trivial but produces working real-time intersection + label updates
evaluationRationale.governanceCompliance: Fully compliant — uses JSXGraph native APIs; InputSanitization (LaTeX→new Function()) boundary preserved
evaluationRationale.ecosystemMaturity: JSXGraph 15+ years of production use; v1.12.2 is stable LTS; no ecosystem risk
evaluationRationale.dlaFit: Most concepts cleanly assigned: IntersectionPoint→DATA, _dep→LOGIC, DragCascadeUpdate→ACTION; SECURITY and LEARN thin in base case
evaluationRationale.forwardPropHealth: IntersectionResolverInterface → NativeIntersectionResolver → JSXGraph _dep → CoordinateLabel → JSXGraphCanvas chain is HEALTHY; TracePath custom update gap noted
evaluationRationale.agentComposability: Core ops composable via IntersectionResolverInterface; TracePath custom board.on('update') handler requires human orchestration
evaluationRationale.prototypeValidation: Not yet tested — score 3 placeholder; prototyper T7 will validate

scenarios.description: Optimistic: perfect reducedUpdate cascade at 60fps+ with all 8 dependents auto-updating. DragBehaviorInterface migration completes with zero regression. New elements (integral, tangent line, second derivative) added with zero handler changes — pure OCP.

assumptions.text: board.reducedUpdate=true + 8 native _dep elements sustains 60fps across Chrome/Firefox/Safari SVG renderers with no dropped frames
DragBehaviorInterface incremental migration completes with zero regression in snap-to-grid and tangency assist — JSXGraph drag callbacks provide same coordinate data as DOM events
All dependent elements declared at board creation time via parentPoint closures — zero ad-hoc DOM mutations, fully ORCH-10 compliant
parentPoint.on('drag') fires within same JSXGraph event loop iteration as functiongraph position — zero frame lag for dependent updates

evaluationRationale.evidenceFit: reducedUpdate cascade correctness at 8+ dependents across all browsers is inference; 60fps guarantee not benchmarked at this scale
evaluationRationale.implementationDifficulty: Trivial once parentPoint pattern established — new dependent elements added via board.create() with zero drag handler changes (pure OCP)
evaluationRationale.riskSeverity: Negligible — JSXGraph single-threaded update loop eliminates race conditions; native cascade is self-maintaining
evaluationRationale.reversibility: Mostly reversible — parentPoint pattern composable; new elements added as dependents are removable without side effects
evaluationRationale.timeToValue: 1-2 weeks after parentPoint migration — new elements declared in board.create() immediately
evaluationRationale.governanceCompliance: Fully compliant — pure JSXGraph native API usage
evaluationRationale.ecosystemMaturity: JSXGraph 15+ years; v1.12.2 stable; native _dep system well-documented
evaluationRationale.dlaFit: All 5 domains cleanly covered: DependencyGraph IS the JSXGraph _dep system — perfect ontology alignment; no domain confusion
evaluationRationale.forwardPropHealth: Full chain: IntersectionResolverInterface → NativeIntersectionResolver → JSXGraph _dep auto-cascade → CoordinateLabel → JSXGraphCanvas FULLY HEALTHY
evaluationRationale.agentComposability: Most operations agent-composable via IntersectionResolverInterface and DragBehaviorInterface; TracePath custom update minor manual gap

scenarios.description: Pessimistic: DragBehaviorInterface migration breaks snap-to-grid and tangency assist. Raw DOM and JSXGraph parentPoint.on('drag') conflict — double-update per frame causes IntersectionPoint flickering. Migration stalls at 6-8 weeks. Teacher demo blocked.

assumptions.text: Raw DOM addEventListener and JSXGraph parentPoint.on('drag') fire in different phases — DOM fires first, updates ty, then JSXGraph re-fires for the same drag event, causing double update
snap-to-grid and tangency assist in dragAssist.ts rely on raw DOM event coordinate APIs not directly available in JSXGraph callback (revised: requires non-trivial refactoring)
Migration timeline extends to 6-8 weeks due to dual-event-system debugging and regression testing of all 16 element types
During migration window, both event systems active — IntersectionPoint visible flicker in teacher demo as board repaints twice per drag frame

contradictions.claim: ev-002 confirms JSXGraph drag callbacks provide full coordinate data — snap-to-grid IS portable to parentPoint.on('drag') without DOM-specific APIs
contradictions.resolution: ROUND 1: ev-002 confirms JSXGraph drag event provides usrCoords. snap-to-grid IS portable — requires refactoring dragAssist.ts to use JXG coordinate system instead of DOM pixel coords. Assumption a2 revised: 'not available' → 'requires ~1-week refactoring'. Risk severity maintained at 2 (significant rework) but worst-case timeline reduced from 8 to 5 weeks. contradictionStatus: resolved.

evaluationRationale.evidenceFit: a3/a4 (migration timeline, flicker) have no direct evidence — inference from DOM/JSXGraph event model; a2 revised after contradiction resolution
evaluationRationale.implementationDifficulty: Difficult — dual-event-system regression + dragAssist.ts refactoring, ~1-2 months total
evaluationRationale.riskSeverity: Severe — IntersectionPoint flickering during teacher live demo is UX-breaking; blocked feature delivery for full teaching feature set
evaluationRationale.reversibility: Partially reversible — DragBehaviorInterface provides abstraction but rollback requires reverting multiple files; significant effort
evaluationRationale.timeToValue: 1-2 months before stable migration — regression debugging delays value delivery
evaluationRationale.governanceCompliance: Mostly compliant — no security gaps; regression is technical, not regulatory
evaluationRationale.ecosystemMaturity: JSXGraph mature; dual-event-system issue is architectural choice, not ecosystem instability
evaluationRationale.dlaFit: Core D/L/A clear; migration risk analysis lacks SECURITY/LEARN coverage; ForwardProp broken during migration window
evaluationRationale.forwardPropHealth: ForwardProp chain broken at ACTION layer during migration: DragCascadeUpdate produces incorrect DragState while dual-event systems coexist
evaluationRationale.agentComposability: Agents cannot compose against unreliable DragState mid-migration; IntersectionPoint coordinates non-deterministic

scenarios.description: Adversarial: |f(x)| absolute value function has a non-differentiable kink at fold boundary. JSXGraph native board.create('intersection') 2-root algorithm may stall or converge to wrong root near the kink. IntersectionResolverInterface fallback path not yet declared — no clean swap exists. Teacher demonstrates live; intersection jumps to wrong coordinate.

assumptions.text: There is NO official JSXGraph wiki example for intersection POINTS of two functiongraphs (claim-023, confidence 0.90). board.create('intersection', [fg1, fg2, 0]) was v1.12.1-improved but is untested for the specific functiongraph types in palantir-math (Linear/Quadratic crossing, |f(x)| crossing linear).

At fold boundary (x where f_raw(x)+ty = 0), the abs-fold creates a kink — the 2-root solver's gradient approximation becomes unreliable at x=fold_boundary. A kink is non-differentiable but continuous; solver may oscillate rather than converge.
IntersectionResolverInterface fallback to JXG.Math.Numerics.root() is not yet declared in the contract layer — no clean swap path exists at the boundary edge case
Teacher drags |f(x)| across fold boundary during live demo; intersection coordinate jumps visibly to wrong position — UX catastrophic for live teaching scenario

contradictions.claim: ev-001 shows JSXGraph intersection uses 2-root algorithm with configurable precision — may handle kinks better than Newton-Raphson alone; kink is a non-differentiable point, not a discontinuity
contradictions.resolution: ROUND 2: ev-001 confirms 2-root algorithm with precision parameter. A kink (|f(x)|) is non-differentiable but continuous — unlike a discontinuity, the solver won't diverge but may oscillate near the kink. Exact behavior at fold boundary is an evidence gap: actual solver behavior on abs functions not documented. NextExperiment exp-jxg-01 created: prototype board.create('intersection') with |linear| function and test coordinate accuracy at fold boundary. contradictionStatus: detected (evidence gap — forwarded to prototyper T7).

evaluationRationale.evidenceFit: Kink behavior at fold boundary is inference; ev-001 provides 2-root algorithm context but not abs-function-specific precision behavior
evaluationRationale.implementationDifficulty: Difficult — custom fallback intersection logic for abs/piecewise + boundary detection + IntersectionResolverInterface fallback declaration, ~1-2 months
evaluationRationale.riskSeverity: Severe — wrong intersection coordinate during teacher live demo is UX-breaking; affects primary use case
evaluationRationale.reversibility: Mostly reversible — IntersectionResolverInterface provides clean fallback swap (JXG.Math.Numerics.root()); adding fallback is 1-2 days once declared
evaluationRationale.timeToValue: 1-2 months — requires prototype validation (T7) to confirm whether the risk is real before building fallback
evaluationRationale.governanceCompliance: Fully compliant — edge case does not affect security boundaries
evaluationRationale.ecosystemMaturity: JSXGraph mature; intersection element precision at abs-function kinks less documented — gap in ecosystem documentation
evaluationRationale.dlaFit: IntersectionSolver as LOGIC with fallback path; adversarial tests the LOGIC→DATA boundary at edge case; SECURITY/LEARN not affected
evaluationRationale.forwardPropHealth: Native path healthy for smooth functions; fallback solver path not declared in IntersectionResolverInterface — gap at edge case boundary
evaluationRationale.agentComposability: Core composable; adversarial edge case requires explicit agent rule for abs-fold boundary detection before composing IntersectionSolver calls
evaluationRationale.prototypeValidation: Not yet tested — score 3 placeholder; T7 MUST test intersection behavior at fold boundary

scenarios.description: Standard adoption: board.on('update') observer + JXG.Math.Numerics.root() warm-start intersection solver + explicit BatchBoardUpdate. Zero migration cost — purely additive. Working intersection + label updates achievable in days. board.reducedUpdate=true CANNOT be used (architectural constraint).

assumptions.text: board.on('update') fires reliably on every JSXGraph board update triggered by functiongraph drag events
JXG.Math.Numerics.root(h, warmStart) with warm start from previous intersection x completes in <1ms for linear and quadratic functiongraph pairs
board.suspendUpdate() + unsuspendUpdate() batches 5 element updates into a single SVG repaint, maintaining 60fps at element counts ≤5
Raw DOM addEventListener stays in place — board.on('update') is purely additive; zero existing behavior affected

contradictions.claim: board.reducedUpdate=true suppresses the full update cycle, preventing board.on('update') from firing — reducedUpdate and board observer are mutually exclusive architectural choices
contradictions.resolution: UNRESOLVABLE: board.reducedUpdate=true suppresses the full board update cycle that board.on('update') depends on. Both confirmed by ev-005. This is an architectural fact: H-B permanently forgoes the 40-60% frame computation reduction that H-A gains from reducedUpdate. Marked unresolvable — evaluator must weigh this against H-B's zero-migration-cost advantage.

evaluationRationale.evidenceFit: board.on('update') confirmed ev-003; suspendUpdate confirmed ev-004; warm-start <1ms from claim-028 (tier-3 inference)
evaluationRationale.implementationDifficulty: Straightforward — additive pattern, zero migration, proven observer+solver combination, ~1-2 weeks
evaluationRationale.riskSeverity: Moderate — board.on('update') DRY risk at 5+ elements (rule of three); performance ceiling without reducedUpdate looms at higher element counts
evaluationRationale.reversibility: Fully reversible — board.on('update') listener removed with zero side effects; zero data loss
evaluationRationale.timeToValue: Days — working intersection + label update prototype achievable in <1 week with pure additive approach
evaluationRationale.governanceCompliance: Mostly compliant — DDD partial violation (board observer is non-JSXGraph vocabulary §DC5-05 flag); no security gaps
evaluationRationale.ecosystemMaturity: JSXGraph mature; board.on('update') well-documented in ev-003
evaluationRationale.dlaFit: Core D/L/A clear; PECS violation: observer mixes SENSE+DECIDE+ACT+LEARN in one callback — domain separation is theoretical without callback decomposition
evaluationRationale.forwardPropHealth: Backend propagation partially designed; observer as implicit hub lacks explicit contract layer between IntersectionSolver and observer callback
evaluationRationale.agentComposability: Core ops composable at solver level; observer callback monolithic — agents cannot compose individual SENSE/DECIDE/ACT operations independently

scenarios.description: Optimistic: observer callback decomposed into 4 typed handlers (IntersectionHandler, LabelHandler, FoldHandler, TelemetryHandler) — each independently composable. Warm-start Newton-Raphson achieves <0.5ms. Full per-frame InteractionTelemetry capture. suspendUpdate eliminates SVG flicker.

assumptions.text: Observer callback decomposed into 4 typed handler functions (IntersectionHandler, LabelHandler, FoldHandler, TelemetryHandler), each with clear D/L/A domain — PECS violation partially mitigated
Warm-start Newton-Raphson (initial guess = previous intersection x) reduces solver iterations by >=50% vs cold start, achieving <0.5ms per intersection solve
Per-frame InteractionTelemetry captures solver iteration counts and element update durations with <0.1ms overhead per frame
board.suspendUpdate() + unsuspendUpdate() fully eliminates visible SVG flicker even with 8 element updates per frame via single-repaint batching

evaluationRationale.evidenceFit: warm-start <0.5ms target is tier-3 inference from claim-028; callback decomposition benefits are inference; other assumptions tier-1/2
evaluationRationale.implementationDifficulty: Straightforward — additive pattern with callback decomposition; zero migration; well-understood observer pattern, ~1-2 weeks
evaluationRationale.riskSeverity: Minor — fine-grained telemetry enables early performance issue detection; decomposed handlers isolate failures
evaluationRationale.reversibility: Fully reversible — remove observer listeners with zero data loss; handler decomposition is pure function extraction
evaluationRationale.timeToValue: Days — zero migration cost; decomposed prototype achievable in <1 week
evaluationRationale.governanceCompliance: Mostly compliant — DDD partial violation persists (board observer vocabulary); no security gaps
evaluationRationale.dlaFit: PECS violation mitigated by decomposition but domain mixing remains at callback registration level — improvement from base but not fully clean
evaluationRationale.forwardPropHealth: Explicit contract for NewtonRaphsonResolver not yet declared even in best case; observer remains implicit hub
evaluationRationale.agentComposability: Decomposed handlers independently composable by agents; handler-level tool bindings achievable; major improvement over base case

scenarios.description: Pessimistic: 10+ dependent elements in board.on('update') observer exceed the 16ms frame budget without reducedUpdate. Observer callback becomes 200-line DRY violation. Performance ceiling hit permanently under H-B — migration toward H-A required to fix.

assumptions.text: Without board.reducedUpdate=true, full JSXGraph board update computes ALL element positions each drag frame — scales linearly with element count
10 element updates × ~1.3ms each = ~13ms computation + ~3ms suspendUpdate repaint = ~16ms total — at exact frame budget limit with zero margin
board.on('update') callback grows to 200+ lines at 10 element types — DRY rule of three triggered at element 3; maintenance burden severe by element 10
No path to adopt reducedUpdate without migrating to H-A parentPoint pattern — performance ceiling is permanent under H-B at 10+ element count

contradictions.claim: ev-004 documents suspendUpdate/unsuspendUpdate for batch updates — this reduces browser layout thrashing and may significantly reduce the frame budget impact
contradictions.resolution: ROUND 1: ev-004 confirms suspendUpdate batches DOM repaints but NOT JSXGraph internal element evaluation. Each element's position IS still computed before the single repaint. suspendUpdate saves ~2-3ms on repaint but computation cost (per-element evaluation) remains. Assumption a2 revised: 10 elements × ~1.3ms + 3ms repaint savings via suspendUpdate → ~13ms total. Tight but potentially within 16ms budget at exactly 10 elements. Risk remains: at 11+ elements or slower hardware, budget exceeded. contradictionStatus: resolved.

evaluationRationale.evidenceFit: suspendUpdate behavior confirmed ev-004; per-element timing and performance ceiling are inference — no direct benchmark
evaluationRationale.implementationDifficulty: Difficult — performance optimization without reducedUpdate requires custom batching research; migration to H-A needed for ceiling fix, ~1-2 months
evaluationRationale.riskSeverity: Critical — 60fps cannot be guaranteed at 10+ elements without reducedUpdate; fundamental performance ceiling blocks full teaching feature set delivery
evaluationRationale.reversibility: Partially reversible — DRY violation requires refactoring; performance fix requires H-A migration; significant effort but data preserved
evaluationRationale.timeToValue: >2 months — performance ceiling prevents full teaching feature delivery until architectural rethink or migration
evaluationRationale.governanceCompliance: Mostly compliant — no compliance gaps; performance ceiling is technical, not regulatory
evaluationRationale.ecosystemMaturity: JSXGraph mature; performance ceiling is architectural choice consequence, not ecosystem instability
evaluationRationale.dlaFit: Observer mixes 4 domains in worst case — D/L/A domain confusion is a defect not just debt at 200+ line callback scale
evaluationRationale.forwardPropHealth: Frame budget exceeded → CoordinateLabel updates stale by 1+ frames → ForwardProp chain delivers incorrect display state to frontend
evaluationRationale.agentComposability: Monolithic 200-line observer; no agent composability; all operations require human orchestration
evaluationRationale.prototypeValidation: Not yet tested — score 3 placeholder; T8 MUST benchmark frame budget at 10 elements

scenarios.description: Adversarial: Raw DOM addEventListener fires, advances ty on DragState. JSXGraph board.on('update') fires in a DIFFERENT event cycle — reads stale ty value, computes intersection with wrong function position. Under rapid drag (20+ events/sec), ty is 2-3 frames stale. Teacher demo: intersection jumps to wrong coordinate mid-drag.

assumptions.text: Raw DOM addEventListener 'mousemove' fires synchronously during drag — updates DragState.ty immediately without triggering board.update()
JSXGraph board.on('update') fires only when JSXGraph's internal board.update() method is called — which is NOT triggered by raw DOM mousemove in the current codebase
Under rapid drag (20+ mousemove events/sec), DOM handler fires 3-4× per frame but board.on('update') fires at most once per board.update() call — 3 intermediate ty values lost
IntersectionSolver in board.on('update') reads DragState.ty after DOM handler has advanced it by 1-2 frames — solver uses stale ty, producing intersection coordinate jump artifacts

contradictions.claim: ev-003 shows board.on('update') fires after board.update() — if raw DOM drag handler internally calls board.update() or triggers a JSXGraph element that calls it, the two may be synchronized
contradictions.resolution: ROUND 2: decision-log confirms current codebase DOM drag handler directly manipulates JXG function parameter (not via board.update()). Therefore DOM drag and board.on('update') ARE in different event systems — synchronization gap is a real architectural risk. The race condition is an architectural fact, not speculation. Evidence gap remains: exact timing behavior under rapid drag (20+ events/sec) with specific hardware timer resolution. NextExperiment exp-jxg-02 created: instrument DOM drag handler + board.on('update') with performance.now() timestamps to measure synchronization gap under rapid drag. contradictionStatus: detected (evidence gap — forwarded to prototyper T8).

evaluationRationale.evidenceFit: DOM/board event system separation confirmed by decision-log analysis and ev-003; race condition timing under rapid drag is inference
evaluationRationale.implementationDifficulty: Extremely hard — resolving DOM↔board event synchronization at <16ms frame boundary requires custom synchronization primitive; research-grade problem
evaluationRationale.riskSeverity: Critical — ty synchronization corruption mid-drag causes intersection coordinate to jump to wrong position during teacher live demo; primary use case failure
evaluationRationale.reversibility: Difficult to reverse — fixing the race condition requires fundamental change to drag event model (migrate toward H-A parentPoint); weeks of architectural rework
evaluationRationale.timeToValue: >2 months — race condition identification + synchronization design + verification under various hardware timer resolutions
evaluationRationale.governanceCompliance: Mostly compliant — no regulatory gaps; race condition is technical architectural failure
evaluationRationale.ecosystemMaturity: JSXGraph mature; race condition is architectural choice consequence, not ecosystem immaturity
evaluationRationale.dlaFit: Race condition exposes ACTION domain split across two event systems — DragState belongs to neither DOM nor board event exclusively; fundamental D/L/A boundary confusion
evaluationRationale.forwardPropHealth: ty synchronization corruption breaks entire propagation chain: DragState → IntersectionSolver → CoordinateLabel → JSXGraphCanvas all receive incorrect values
evaluationRationale.agentComposability: Agents cannot compose against a race condition; any IntersectionSolver call during rapid drag yields unpredictable results; no coordination protocol defined
evaluationRationale.prototypeValidation: Not yet tested — score 3 placeholder; T8 MUST instrument drag event timing to confirm or deny race condition

phase1_golden_tables.description: BunStateFile (BunFile lazy-load) + StateSchemaDefinition (TypeBox Struct) establish correct DATA shape. All DATA objects classified with evidence.
gaps: gap-s1-01: Bun.write atomicity unconfirmed — temp-file rename pattern should be added before Phase 1 is declared complete

phase2_operational.description: StateReducer (DC5-06) added for LOGIC layer. SchemaValidatorInterface enables TypeBox/Valibot swap via OCP. StateChangeAuditLog captures write events.
gaps: FileSystemAccessPolicy POSIX permission enforcement not formalized
CLI 'state read' command surface not declared

phase3_ai_first.description: Agent composability requires locking protocol for concurrent AtomicWriteAction calls. Without locking, sc-s1-01-adv (concurrent write corruption) blocks Phase 3.
keyDeliverables: Advisory file lock mechanism for AtomicWriteAction
TypeBox JSON Schema → CLI help text integration
gaps: Concurrent write coordination protocol (blocks Agent Composability score >= 4)
sc-s1-01-adv remains unresolvable without AtomicWriteAction redesign

phase1_golden_tables.description: StateChangeAuditLog as primary DATA artifact (actually LEARN, which makes this LEARN-first). AppendLogAction (FileSink) + explicit .flush() establishes correct persistence shape. StateSchemaDefinition (Zod) defines entry schema.
gaps: gap-s1-01: FileSink flush latency under load unconfirmed — needs bun bench

phase2_operational.description: StateReducer added to replay log and derive current state (canonical focus value). Compaction strategy implemented. SchemaValidatorInterface wraps Zod.
gaps: gap-s1-02: Replay latency at 50k entries unconfirmed

phase3_ai_first.description: Append-only log is inherently agent-composable. Multiple agents can AppendLogAction simultaneously without coordination. StateReducer provides query surface for agent tools.
gaps: sc-s1-02-adv remains unresolvable at log scale — compaction strategy must be production-hardened before Phase 3 at scale

simulationRuns.note: sc-jxg-A-adv contradiction remains 'detected' — evidence gap at abs-fold boundary forwarded to prototyper T7
sc-jxg-B-base reducedUpdate mutual exclusivity is unresolvable architectural fact. sc-jxg-B-adv DOM/board race condition is evidence gap forwarded to prototyper T8

Worst-case timeline: 8 weeks → 5 weeks. contradictionStatus: detected→resolved.

revisionHistory.trigger: REQUIRED — sc-jxg-A-adv and sc-jxg-B-adv have contradictionStatus='detected' (evidence gaps) after Round 1
changesApplied: sc-jxg-A-adv: Evidence gap refined using ev-001 2-root algorithm context. Kink is non-differentiable but continuous — solver won't diverge but may oscillate. Exact fold-boundary behavior unconfirmed. NextExperiment exp-jxg-01 created. contradictionStatus: stays 'detected' (forwarded to prototyper T7 for prototype validation).

sc-jxg-B-adv: Race condition confirmed as architectural fact using decision-log analysis: DOM drag does NOT call board.update(). DOM drag and board.on('update') ARE in different event systems. Evidence gap remains: exact timing under rapid drag. NextExperiment exp-jxg-02 created. contradictionStatus: stays 'detected' (forwarded to prototyper T8).
sc-jxg-A-adv: forwardPropHealth score confirmed at 3 (fallback path not yet declared in IntersectionResolverInterface); reversibility confirmed at 4 (clean swap via interface once declared).
sc-jxg-B-best: PECS mitigation pattern formalized — decompose observer into 4 typed handlers; dlaFit confirmed at 3 (improvement from base but not fully clean).
contradictionsRemaining: contra-jxg-A-adv-01 (evidence gap — prototyper T7 will test)
contra-jxg-B-adv-01 (evidence gap — prototyper T8 will test)
evidenceGapsIdentified: gap-jxg-01 confirmed: need prototype test of board.create('intersection') at |linear| fold boundary
gap-jxg-02 confirmed: need timing instrumentation of DOM drag vs board.on('update') under rapid drag
newExperiments: exp-jxg-01: Prototype board.create('intersection', [abs_fg, linear_fg]) and drag through fold boundary — measure coordinate accuracy before/at/after fold point
exp-jxg-02: Instrument DOM drag handler + board.on('update') with performance.now() — measure timing gap under rapid drag (20+ events/sec); confirm or deny race condition

nextExperiments.description: Confirm Bun.write() atomicity: write a test JSON file and send kill -9 signal mid-write. Check if partial file results. Run 100 iterations.
nextExperiments.expectedResolution: If no partial file produced for < 4KB writes: Bun.write atomic for small CLI state → sc-s1-01-base and sc-s1-01-worst risk profile improves

nextExperiments.description: Benchmark StateReducer replay: generate 10k, 50k, 100k LogEntry records and measure Bun replay P50/P99 latency. Compare against 100ms startup budget.
nextExperiments.expectedResolution: If P99 < 100ms for 50k entries: H2 worst-case severity improves; if P99 > 500ms: confirms sc-s1-02-adv risk profile

nextExperiments.description: Prototype: implement board.create('intersection', [abs_fg, linear_fg]) where abs_fg = board.create('functiongraph', [x => Math.abs(x + parentPoint.Y())]) and drag parentPoint through fold boundary (ty crosses 0). Measure intersection coordinate accuracy at 10 points near fold. Pass criteria: coordinate error < 0.01 at all 10 test points. KEY EVIDENCE: claim-023 (confidence 0.90) — no official wiki example for this exact pattern; v1.12.1 improved curve-curve precision (claim-009) but behavior at abs-fold kink is unconfirmed.
nextExperiments.expectedResolution: If coordinates accurate at fold boundary: sc-jxg-A-adv riskSeverity improves 2→4; native intersection element safe for abs functions. If coordinates wrong: IntersectionResolverInterface fallback path must be declared before H-A adoption.

nextExperiments.description: Instrument prototype: add performance.now() timestamps to both raw DOM addEventListener callback and board.on('update') callback. Simulate rapid drag (requestAnimationFrame loop firing 20 events/sec). Measure: (a) time gap between DOM event and next board.on('update'); (b) whether ty read in board.on('update') matches ty set in last DOM event.
nextExperiments.expectedResolution: If ty values are always synchronized: sc-jxg-B-adv riskSeverity improves 1→3; race condition is theoretical only. If ty gap detected: race condition is confirmed; H-B architecture has a critical production risk under rapid drag.

unresolved.description: StateReducer replay latency at 50,000 entries not benchmarked. Flagged for evaluator. NextExperiment exp-s1-02 created.
AtomicWriteAction concurrent writes require locking not provided by Bun.write. This is an architectural fact. Evaluator should note this as a hard requirement for Phase 3 (Agent Teams) use of H1.
H2 compaction inherits H1 atomicity risk. Any system requiring compaction at scale cannot escape Bun.write atomicity concern. Evaluator should note this as a shared risk.

JSXGraph 2-root algorithm behavior at |f(x)| fold boundary (kink, non-differentiable point) not confirmed. Forwarded to prototyper T7 via exp-jxg-01. Evaluator should flag hyp-jxg-A as conditional on prototype validation — if T7 finds intersection coordinate error > 0.01 at fold boundary, IntersectionResolverInterface fallback path must be declared before H-A adoption.

board.reducedUpdate=true and board.on('update') are mutually exclusive: reducedUpdate suppresses the update cycle that the observer depends on. This is an architectural fact confirmed by ev-005. H-B permanently forgoes 40-60% frame computation reduction that H-A achieves via reducedUpdate. Evaluator must weigh against H-B's zero-migration-cost advantage.

DOM drag + board.on('update') race condition confirmed as architectural risk (separate event systems per decision-log). Exact timing impact under rapid drag (20+ events/sec) not measured. Forwarded to prototyper T8 via exp-jxg-02. Evaluator should note: if T8 confirms ty synchronization gap, sc-jxg-B-adv riskSeverity=1 is confirmed and H-B should be rated HIGH risk for production teaching demo use.

t5Status.note: Hypotheses drafted by ontologist (T4), formalized by simulator (T5). Moved from hypotheses_jsxgraph_sequencer_001_PENDING_SIMULATOR to main hypotheses array.

t6Status.winRationale: H-A scores higher on ForwardPropHealth (4 vs 3), DLA-Fit (4/5 vs 3), AgentComposability (3/4 vs 3/4), and eliminates the critical reducedUpdate mutual exclusivity that caps H-B at <10 elements. H-A's adversarial risk (fold boundary precision) is smaller scope and more targeted than H-B's adversarial risk (DOM/board race condition — architectural debt). Migration cost is H-A's only real disadvantage; DragBehaviorInterface mitigates this.
